Re: 
Opinion No. 83-1008; Our 
Advice No. A-83-292 
Dear l<lr. Rocne: 
You nave requested the Commission's input regarding Attorney 
General's Opinlon No. 83-1008 relating to possiole conflicts of 
interest involving tne Yuba County water Agency. 
We will 
address only tne issues raised under tne Political Reform 
ACt..!/ (the "Act"). 
Tne materlal facts presented for our 
analysis are as follows:ll 
FACTS 
Tne Yuoa County Water Agency (the "Agency") is an "agency" 
witnin the meaning of tne Act,17 whose board memoers are 
"pUblic officials" wi tnin the meaning of the Act.Y 
The 
Agency board conSlsts of the five members of tne Yuoa County 
Board of Supervisors plus two representatives from tne member 
units, selected oy tne Advisory Council. 
The Advisory Council 
II Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 
II If this were a direct request to us for advice 
pursuant to Sectlon 83114, we would require more factual detail 
before rendering specific advice. 
~I Section 82003. 
il Section 82048. 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page 2 
is comprised of memoers of tne boards of directors of the member 
units. 
The two non-superv~sorial positions on the Agency's 
boara must be selected by the Advisory Council from its 
membership. 
Consequently, two members of the Agency's board 
must be members of the member units' boards. 
Among the member districts of the Agency are the following: 
Irrigation Districts 
South Yuba water District 
Wheatland Water District 
Brophy Water District 
Municipal and Industr~al 
Olivenurst Public utility District 
Linda County Water District 
City of Wheatland 
The Agency sells water to the member districts and has the 
power to construct water storage, flood control and power 
generat~on facilities and has done so in constructing the New 
Bullards Bar Dam. 
In addition, the Agency has been planning to 
construct a cana~ taking water from the Yuba River to the 
southern part of the County for sale to the three water 
distr~cts organized in that area.~/ Recently two of the 
districts 7/ have expressed a desire to withdraw trom the joint 
project, leaving only the Wheatland Water District still 
interested. 
Apparently this has caused some strife amongst the 
various districts involved, as evidenced by the October 26, 
1983, letter from Paul R. Minasian, attorney. for the South Yuba 
Water District, to your office. 
The three districts are 
apparently battling for water allocation. 
The Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency 
consists of the following seven members, described as follows in 
5/ 
This canal construction project would be financed by a 
PL984 Federal Loan. 
~ South Yuoa Water District, Brophy Water District and 
Wheatland Water District. 
2/ 
South Yuoa Water District and Brophy Water District. 


Clayton P. Roche 
January 4, 1984 
Page 3 
Mr. Mlnasian's letters (including bacKground information as to 
their relationsnips wlth water lssues facing the Agency) • 
1. 
Michael Rue lS a Director and President of the 
Board of South Yuoa Water District, elected by the 
[Advisory Council] to tne Yuba County Water Agency. 
He 
is, of course, a landowner withln South Yuba Water 
Distrlct. 
2. 
Doug Waltz, Supervisor In Yuba County, and 
also a landowner witnin the Wneatland Water Distrlct. 
Mr. Waltz is the brother of a director of the Wheatland 
Water Distrlct, Price Waltz.~/ 
3. 
Sam Shintafter, a landowner within the 
Hallwood Water Company, a member unit holding a 
contract for waters from the Yuba River which lies 
north of the Rlver. 
The amount or quantity of waters 
which are con~acted for by tne Yuba County Water 
Agency south of Yuba River may in the future have some 
impact upon the abllity of Hallwood Water Company to 
purcnase water under a clause in their water contract 
permitting them to so purchase and to the application 
of aeficiency clauses during dry periods. 
The 
application of those deficiency clauses will probably 
be more strict and occur more often after contracts are 
entered into for water service in the South County 
area. 
~/ It should be noted here that the fact they are 
orothers lS not a basis for a conflict of interest under the 
Act. 
Tnis fact is included here only because it has been raised 
in the correspondence and rather than ignore it, we decided to 
formally lay it to rest. 
In addition to the facts provided in 
Mr. Minaslan's letter, Yuba County Counsel Dennis Barlow 
provides the followlng salient information regarding Mr. Waltz. 
He "is a rice grower and owner of substantial property within 
the Wheatland Water District. 
"The actions of the Agency in 
securing a [PL984] loan for a canal system and then selling the 
water to the [D]istrict which would in turn develop a 
distrioution system and sell water to the individual landowners 
on tne dlstrlct WOUld be ot benefit "to him in that his costs in 
securing water would be decreased." 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page 4 
4. 
Charles Center, a memoer of tne Board of 
Supervisors, who lives and owns property within the 
ooundaries of the Olivehurst Public Utility District. 
The costs of water to tne Olivehurst Puolic Utility 
District, Linda County Water District, South Yuoa, 
Brophy or Wheatland or the City of Wheatland, would all 
vary to some degree with the question of whether or not 
South YuOa and Brophy are denied a water contract from 
the river or whether they are required to participate 
1n tne PL984 project. 
Further, the question of 
allocation of amounts of water among these districts 
will nave a dramatic affect [sic] upon the costs of 
tneir water service. 
5. 
John Dower, Supervisor from the Marysville 
area, is a landowner witnin the City of Marysville. 
The City ouys water under a contract with tne Agency 
for recreational filling of a laKe. 
6. 
Mr. McGill is a Supervisor, and a resident 
landowner within tne YuOa County Water District, a 
Ca11forn1a water d1str1ct within YuOa County. 
This 
District does not presently have [aJ contract for 
water, out has oeen allocated a large amount of water 
oy tne Agency under 1tS minutes in the past. 
Any water 
contract entered 1nto in the South County area affects 
the chances that the Agency will live up to its 
or1ginal premises or will apply the deficiency clause 
on a str1ct oasis Should a contract Oe entered into 
hereafter with YuOa County Water District. 
7. 
Mr. Devereaux 1S the Supervisor from the Linda 
area and we are informed that he lives and owns land 
witnin the Ooundar1es of the Linda County Water 
[District]. 
Under the PL984 project, this District, 
liKe the Olivenurst puolic Utility District, would 
oOtain suOstantial amounts of water with no financial 
contrioution or guarantees until those agencies elected 
to taKe water from the project. 
QUESTIONS 
Questions have been presented to you regarding at least two 
of these Ooard memOers. 
The two general questions asked oy Yuoa 
County Counsel Dennis A. Barlow are as follows: 


Clayton P. Rocne 
Ja nuar:y 4 I 
1984 
Page 5 
1. 
Maya airector of the Yuba County Water Agency 
who is also a director of a California Water pistrict 
lawfully act on matters which directly affect the water 
dis tr ic t? 
2. 
Maya director of the Yuba County Water Agency 
wno owns land w~thin a 
Californ~a Water District 
lawfully participate ~n Agency actions whicn will 
provide water to such district? 
ANALYSIS 
Tne Act prohioits officials from acting on matters in which 
tney are financially interested. 
Section 87100 states: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government snall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use n~s official position to influence a 
governmental dec~sion in whicn he knows or has reason 
to know he nas a financial interest. 
A financial interest is defined in Section 87103, which 
reads as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
w~thin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeaole tnat the dec~sion will have a material 
financ~al effect, distinguisnaole from its effect on 
the puol~c generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more tnan one tnousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest wortn 
more tnan one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a 
commerc~al lending institution in the regular 
course ot business on terms available to the 
puolic witnout regard to offic~al status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or 
more in value provided to, received by or promised 
to the pUblic official within 12 months prior to 
tne time when the decision is made. 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page 6 
(d) 
Any business entity in wnich the pUDlic 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on Dehalf of a public official, or by a 
Dusiness entity or trust in wnicn the official, tne 
otficlal's agents, spouse, and dependent children own 
directly, indirectly, or Deneficially a 10-percent 
interest or greater. 
In order for any of tne directors of the Agency to have a 
conflict of interest~1 whicn would require disqualification, 
the tests set forth in Section 87103 must be met, i.e.: 
1. 
There must exist one or more of the four specified 
flnanclal relationships; 
2. 
There must be a reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
flowing from tne official action; 
3. 
The effect must be material as to the company in which 
the investment is held, the real property in which an interest 
is held, the source of income, or the business entity of which 
the official is a partner, officer, employee, etc. 
4. 
The material financial effect upon any of the above must 
be distinguishable from its effect upon the public generally. 
The "public generally" has been defined to include a 
"signiflcant segment of the pUbllC generally." 
2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18703.lQ/ 
~ We stress, again, that we are only considering issues 
raised under the Politlcal Reform Act and only the facts 
presented here. 
1£1 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 states: 
A material financial effect of a governmental 
decision on an official's interests, as described in 
Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d), is 
distlnguisnable from its effect on the pUblic generally 
unless the decision will affect the official's interest 
(continued next page) 


Clayton P. Roche 
January 4, 1984 
Page 7 
Respondlng to the ~lrst question posed by Mr. Barlow, there 
is no basis under tne Act for a conflict of interest. 
The 
directors of the Agency whO are selected from the directors of 
the member unlts (water/irrigation districts) have no investment 
in tne districts (they are not business entitiesll/), the 
districts are not an interest in real property of the board 
memoers, nor are the districts a source of income121 or a 
Dusiness entity for which they are a director, etc. 
None of the 
bases for satisfying the first test, discussed above, is met. 
Consequently, tne fact that there may De a reasonaoly 
foreseeaDle material tlnanclal effect upon the district is not 
sufficient to give rise to the requirement of disqualification 
unaer Sections 87100 and 87103. 
Responding to Mr. Barlow's second question, there is the 
potentlal tnat any or all of tne Agency's directors at some time 
may De required to disqualify themselves because an agency 
(Footnote 10, continued) 
in substantlally the same manner as it will affect all 
members of the public or a Significant segment of the 
pUD11C. 
Except as provlded herein, an industry, trade 
or profession does not constitute a significant segment 
of the general public. 
(a) 
In the case of an elected state officer, an 
industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant 
segment of the pUDlic generally. 
(b) 
In the case of any other elected official, an 
industry, trade or profession of which that official is 
a member may constitute a significant segment of the 
pUDlic generally if that industry, trade or profession 
is a predominant industry, trade or profession in the 
official's jurisdiction or in the district represented 
DY the officia 1. 
* * * 
III BUSlness entities must De operated for profit. 
Section 82005. 
121 
Section 82030 (b) (2) excludes from the term "income" 
salary and reimoursement for expenses received by an official 
from a governmental entity. 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page 8 
dec~sion will affect their ~nterests ~n a material manner whicn 
is d~st~nguisnanle from the effect on the pUblic generally. 
In the case of Director Waltz, he apparently owns a large 
amount of land wnich needs water tor the purpose of growing 
rice. 
Onv~ous1y, he nas an interest in real property worth more 
than $1,000 and an investment in nis rice growing business worth 
more than $1,000. 
Presumably his rice growing business is a 
source of income of $250 to him and he is probably an officer, 
director, partner, etc., of the business. 
Consequently, the 
first of tne tests is met. 
It is alleged that there will be a 
financial e~fect upon Mr. Waltz' business which will be a 
reasonably foreseeable result of the decision, i.e., lowered 
water COSts. 
We are g~ven no facts to establish tne magnitude 
of the effect to determine it it is material under the 
gu~aelines establisned by our regulat~on, 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18702. 
Assuming that the materiality test is met, we 
then must aaaress the "pUblic generally" question. 
The test is 
articulated in our regula tio n, 2 Ca 1. Adm. Code Section 18703, 
quoted in relevant part in footnote 10, supra. 
The Commission has determined on numerous occasions that the 
public generally ~s the public ~n the ent~re jurisdiction. 13/ 
The Jurisdiction of the Agency is apparently the entire County 
and also inclUdes tne jurisdictions of all of the member units, 
not Just the Wheatland Water District.l!/ 
An industry, such 
as rice grow~ng, cannot be cons~dered a significant segment of 
the pUbl~c generally unless it is the predominant industry in 
the Jurisdiction. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 (b). 
Even 
then, tnis exception to the general rule that an industry is not 
a significant segment of the public generally applies only if---
the offic~al ~s an elected official. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703(b). 
While Mr. Waltz is an elected official when he serves 
as a member of the Board of Supervisors of Yuba County, he is 
not elected to the Board ot the Agency, where he serves 
~/ Jurisdiction is defined in Section 82035. 
In 
relevant part, it states: 
"Jurisaiction" means the state with respect to a state 
agency and, with respect to a local government agency, 
the region, county, city, district or other 
geographical area in which it has Jurisdiction. 
14/ 
Water Code Appendix, Section 84-1. 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page ~ 
ex officio. ls/ 
Consequently, even if rice growing was a 
predom~nant ~ndustry within tne jurisdiction of the Agency, the 
exception in Section 18703(b) would not apply in this 
c~rcumstance. 
As a result, Mr. Waltz' ousiness and land would have to be 
affected by the decision "in suostantially the same manner as it 
will affect all memoers of the public [in the Agency's 
jurisdiction] or a Significant segment of tne pUblic" (2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18703) 
~n order for there to be no requirement 
of d~squalif~cation. 
We are told that he will be affected in a similar fashion to 
most other lanaowners in the Wheatland Water District. 
We have 
insufficient ~nformation to determine if this group would 
constitute a "Significant segment" of the pUblic for the 
Agency. 
However, it is unlikely that large landowners 
(presumbly r~ce growers or otner members of the agri-ousiness 
industry) would meet tne tests for diversity established in the 
Comm~ssion's op~n~ons on this topic. 16/ 
Furthermore, if those 
wno would be affected "in suostantially the same manner" as 
Mr. Waltz w~ll each be affected in that manner because they are 
large growers, then tney would constitute an "industry, trade or 
protession"17/ thereby precluding them from constituting a 
"s~gnificant segment of the PUblic generally.,,18/ 
15/ Water Code Appendix, Section" 84-7(a). 
We have made a 
similar determination in our interpretation of Section 84308 of 
the Act. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.1. 
16/ 
See the following published opinions of the 
Commission (copies enclosed for your convenience) : 
Owen, 2 FPPC Ops. 77, No. 76-005 
Ferraro, 4 FPPC Ops. 62, No. 78-009 
Gilmor, 3 FPPC. Ops. 38, No. 76-089 
0gelsbY, 1 FPPC Ops. 71, No. 75-083 
Overstreet, 6 FPPC Ops. 12, No. 80-010 
17/ 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. 
~/ See Ferraro Opinion, supra, fn. 16, and 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 1~703. 


Clayton P. Rocne 
January 4, 1984 
Page 10 
It snould oe pOinted out that this analysis has not focused 
on any potential Section 1090 issues which may be raised by this 
request as that is your agency's province. 
Furthermore, we have 
not addressed in any detail the potential confllcts of interest 
under the Political Reform Act for other members of the Agency's 
board, such as Mr. Shintaffer, Mr. Center and Mr. Devereaux, to 
name Just a few, who would appear to oe in somewhat similar 
situations to tnat of Mr. Waltz. 
The analysis as to each of 
tneir situations would parallel that above. 
However, we have no 
lnformation on wnether they are rice growers, farmers, or merely 
homeowners. 
Obviously, the materlal facts are necessary to a 
full analysis of their duties under the Act. 
Should you obtain additional information on any of the 
members' situations, including Mr. Waltz', we would be pleased 
to review those new facts and to provide our input to you.

(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more tnan one tnousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b)

by a

puolic witnout regard to offic~al status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or

tne time when the decision is made.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether directors of the Yuba County Water Agency who are also directors of or property owners within member water districts have conflicts of interest when the Agency votes on matters affecting those districts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Directors who serve on both the Agency and member district boards do not have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act because the districts themselves are not financial interests as defined by the Act. However, directors who own property within districts may have conflicts depending on whether the Agency decision has a material financial effect on their real property distinguishable from the public generally.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document appears to be truncated mid-conclusion. Full text of second question analysis and final conclusion are cut off. FPPC response letter dated January 4, 1984, responding to Attorney General's Opinion No. 83-1008. Contains detailed factual background on seven board members and their potential conflicts.

CONCLUSION: Responding to the first question posed by Mr. Barlow, there is no basis under the Act for a conflict of interest. The directors of the Agency who are selected from the directors of the member units (water/irrigation districts) have no investment in the districts (they are not business entities), the districts are not an interest in real property of the board members, nor are the districts a source of income or a business entity for which they are a director, etc.

Tne materlal facts presented for our analysis are as follows:ll FACTS Tne Yuoa County Water Agency (the "Agency") is an "agency" witnin the meaning of tne Act,17 whose board memoers are "pUblic officials" wi tnin the meaning of the Act.Y The Agency board conSlsts of the five members of tne Yuoa County Board of Supervisors plus two representatives from tne member units, selected oy tne Advisory Council. The two non-superv~sorial positions on the Agency's boara must be selected by the Advisory Council from its membership. Consequently, two members of the Agency's board must be members of the member units' boards. Among the member districts of the Agency are the following: Irrigation Districts South Yuba water District Wheatland Water District Brophy Water District Municipal and Industr~al Olivenurst Public utility District Linda County Water District City of Wheatland The Agency sells water to the member districts and has the power to construct water storage, flood control and power generat~on facilities and has done so in constructing the New Bullards Bar Dam. In addition, the Agency has been planning to construct a cana~ taking water from the Yuba River to the southern part of the County for sale to the three water distr~cts organized in that area.~/ Recently two of the districts 7/ have expressed a desire to withdraw trom the joint project, leaving only the Wheatland Water District still interested. Apparently this has caused some strife amongst the various districts involved, as evidenced by the October 26, 1983, letter from Paul R. embedding.summary: The FPPC advises on conflicts of interest for Yuba County Water Agency board members under the Political Reform Act, addressing whether directors who also serve on member water districts or own property within those districts have disqualifying conflicts when voting on Agency matters.
